questions.question: What does research show about adventure sports and mental health benefits?
options: No evidence — adventure sports only cause injuries

Moderate: flow states reliably produced (associated with wellbeing), self-efficacy through mastery, controlled fear confrontation teaches anxiety regulation; adventure therapy shows moderate-to-strong effects for anxiety/PTSD/depression with professional facilitation
Strong evidence proves extreme sports cure all mental illness
Only professional athletes benefit from adventure sports
questions.explanation: Evidence is moderate: (1) Flow states — adventure sports consistently produce flow (complete absorption, skill-challenge match) associated with life satisfaction and reduced rumination, (2) Self-efficacy — mastery builds confidence transferring to other domains, (3) Fear management — controlled confrontation (climbing while scared but safe) teaches anxiety regulation, (4) Adventure therapy — professionally-guided activities show moderate-to-strong effects for anxiety, PTSD, depression, substance use. CRITICAL: benefits require skill development and safety protocols — reckless risk-taking creates trauma, not wellbeing.

questions.question: What distinguishes healthy calculated risk from dangerous recklessness in adventure sports?
options: All adventure sports are reckless

Calculated risk: proper training, appropriate safety equipment used correctly, knowledge of hazards, risk management protocols, respecting personal limits, gradual progression; Reckless: self-taught, skipping safety gear, ignoring hazards, ego-driven beyond skill level, using risk to self-harm
Only professionals can engage in calculated risk
Recklessness and adventure sports are the same thing
questions.explanation: Calculated risk (healthy): (1) Proper training/instruction before attempting, (2) Appropriate safety equipment (helmet, harness, PFD) used correctly, (3) Knowledge of objective hazards (weather, avalanche danger, water levels), (4) Risk management (checking gear, communication plans, retreat protocols), (5) Respect personal limits (not pushing beyond skill for ego/peer pressure), (6) Gradual progression. Reckless: attempting without training, skipping safety gear, ignoring hazards, no backup plans, ego-driven difficulty jumps, using risk as suicide proxy or to 'feel alive' when numb. Ask sport community: 'Is this responsible?' — if consensus is 'sketchy,' it's reckless.

questions.question: How do adventure sports reliably produce flow states, and why does this matter for mental health?
options: Flow states don't occur in adventure sports

High-consequence activities demand total attention (can't ruminate while climbing/skiing); skill-challenge match creates narrow band where challenge slightly exceeds skill = flow (complete absorption, time distortion, effortless action, intrinsic reward); flow associated with life satisfaction, reduced depression, wellbeing
Only meditation produces flow states
Flow states are dangerous and should be avoided
questions.explanation: Adventure sports reliable flow triggers because: (1) Demand total present-moment attention (can't safely navigate technical climb/steep ski while mentally elsewhere), (2) Clear goals/feedback (route/line to complete, immediate feedback from body/environment), (3) Skill-challenge match (too easy = boredom, too hard = anxiety; flow in narrow band where challenge slightly exceeds skill). Flow characteristics: time distortion, loss of self-consciousness, effortless performance, intrinsic reward. Mental health benefit: flow associated with life satisfaction, reduced depression/rumination, enhanced wellbeing. Flow research shows adventure sports among most reliable triggers.

questions.question: What is adventure therapy, and how does it differ from recreational adventure sports?
options: Adventure therapy and recreational sports are identical

Adventure therapy = professionally-facilitated use of challenge activities (ropes courses, climbing, wilderness expeditions) as therapeutic intervention with trained therapists, intentional processing, safety prioritization; shows moderate-to-strong evidence for anxiety, PTSD, depression; differs from recreational (no professional facilitation/processing)
Adventure therapy means doing extreme sports alone to treat mental illness
Adventure therapy has no evidence base
questions.explanation: Adventure therapy: professionally-facilitated challenge activities (therapeutic ropes courses, wilderness therapy, adjunct climbing programs) as mental health intervention. Critical elements: (1) Professional facilitation (therapists trained in adventure skills AND mental health treatment), (2) Intentional processing (debriefing for therapeutic insight, not just 'fun'), (3) Safety prioritized (lower-risk versions like top-rope climbing). Evidence: moderate-to-strong for adolescent behavioral issues, self-esteem, depression, anxiety; emerging PTSD research. Differs from recreational: recreational lacks professional facilitation/therapeutic processing. NOT self-guided — self-prescribing risky activities as 'therapy' dangerous.

questions.question: What are major accessibility barriers to adventure sports, and what solutions exist?
options: Adventure sports are universally accessible with no barriers

Barriers: high cost (skiing $2K+/season, climbing $1K+ gear, surfing coastal premium), socioeconomic inequity, injury risk, geographic access; Solutions: used gear, bouldering gyms ($15 drop-in), trail running (lowest cost), resort work (free passes), adaptive sports programs (disabilities), budget alternatives, community programs/scholarships
Only wealthy people are interested in adventure sports
Accessibility doesn't matter for outdoor activities
questions.explanation: Major barriers: (1) Cost — skiing $2,000+/season, climbing gear $1,000+, mountain bikes $2K-10K, surfing coastal living premium, (2) Socioeconomic — benefits concentrated in higher-income populations (equity issue), (3) Geographic — mountains, oceans, trails not equally distributed, (4) Disability — many activities assumed able-bodied only. Solutions: used gear (Craigslist, REI sales), bouldering gyms (drop-in $15-25), trail running (shoes only, lowest cost), resort work (free lift tickets), adaptive sports programs (sit-skiing, adaptive climbing, modified equipment), community scholarships, budget entry points. Systemic issue: outdoor recreation access tied to privilege.
